Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

- World model: Level 1 has a dark gray background with white regions. Key objects: (1) A horizontal black bar at rows 28-31 with a yellow segment at cols 38-39, (2) Two blue 3x4 blocks on the right edge at rows 24-27 and 32-35, (3) A charcoal shape with yellow bar at rows 44-49. Pink bar at top is likely HUD. Only MOUSE action available.
- Goal model: Unknown - need to probe by clicking on objects to understand mechanics.
- Plan: Click on the yellow segment in the black bar first to test interactivity.

end of world model.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after clicking on the yellow segment. I need to compare the before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the click
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

print("=== BEFORE ===")
for n in before_seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\n=== AFTER ===")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check for differences
print("\n=== DIFF ===")
before_colors = {n['id']: n['color'] for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_colors = {n['id']: n['color'] for n in after_seg['nodes']}
before_pixels = {n['id']: n['pixels'] for n in before_seg['nodes']}
after_pixels = {n['id']: n['pixels'] for n in after_seg['nodes']}

for nid in set(list(before_colors.keys()) + list(after_colors.keys())):
    bc = before_colors.get(nid, 'N/A')
    ac = after_colors.get(nid, 'N/A')
    bp = before_pixels.get(nid, 'N/A')
    ap = after_pixels.get(nid, 'N/A')
    if bc != ac or bp != ap:
        print(f"  Node {nid}: color {bc}->{ac}, pixels {bp}->{ap}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The diff is confusing because node IDs shifted. Let me compare by color and pixel count instead to understand what actually changed on the board.
BEFORE:
- P: 64 pixels (top bar)
- G: 2580 pixels (main background)
- W: 848 pixels (upper right white)
- b: 16 pixels (blue block top right)
- B: 72 pixels (black bar left)
- Y: 8 pixels (yellow in black bar)
- B: 96 pixels (black bar right)
- W: 368 pixels (lower right white)
- b: 16 pixels (blue block lower right)
- c: 16 pixels (charcoal shape)
- Y: 12 pixels (yellow bar lower)
AFTER:
- P: 63 pixels (top bar - shrunk by 1!)
- c: 1 pixel (new tiny charcoal at [0, 63])
- G: 2580 pixels (main background - same)
- W: 848 pixels (upper right white - same)
- b: 16 pixels (blue block top right - same)
- B: 72 pixels (black bar left - same)
- Y: 8 pixels (yellow in black bar - same)
- B: 96 pixels (black bar right - same)
- W: 368 pixels (lower right white - same)
- b: 16 pixels (blue block lower right - same)
- c: 16 pixels (charcoal shape - same)
- Y: 12 pixels (yellow bar lower - same)
So the ONLY change is:
1. The pink bar at top went from 64 to 63 pixels (lost 1 pixel)
2. A new 1-pixel charcoal appeared at [0, 63]
This means the pink bar is indeed a timer/counter that decreases with each action. The actual gameplay objects didn't change at all! Clicking on the yellow segment didn't do anything meaningful.
Let me try clicking on other objects. The blue blocks or the charcoal shape might be interactive. Let me try the blue block first.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The pink bar at top is a timer that shrinks by 1 per action. Clicking on the yellow segment in the black bar had NO effect on gameplay objects - only the timer decreased. All game objects remain unchanged.
Goal model: Still unknown. Need to find which objects are interactive.
Plan: Try clicking on the blue blocks (right edge) and the charcoal shape to find interactive elements.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the upper blue block (rows 24-27, cols 60-63)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 61}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
